mnemonics.title: Three-Way Handshake Steps
mnemonics.content: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK (Remember: 'SYN, SYN-Acknowledge, Acknowledge' or 'Saying Yes to Connect' - Client initiates, Server confirms, Client confirms back)

mnemonics.title: Connectionless vs Connection-Oriented
mnemonics.content: UDP = Unreliable Datagram Protocol (Connectionless), TCP = Transmission Control Protocol (Connection-Oriented). Remember: 'U' for Unreliable/UDP, 'T' for Trust/TCP

acronyms.usage: Connectionless transport layer protocol - used for DNS, video streaming, VoIP
Connection-oriented transport layer protocol - used for HTTP, FTP, email, SSH
First packet in TCP three-way handshake to initiate connection
Packet confirming receipt of data in TCP
Identifier used in virtual circuit networks to route packets

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a phone call: Person A calls (SYN) → Person B answers and says 'Hello' (SYN-ACK) → Person A confirms 'Hello' (ACK). Only after this conversation, actual talking (data transfer) begins.
Think of postal mail - each letter has complete address (source & destination) and travels independently. No prior arrangement needed with post office.
Think of booking a flight - you reserve a specific route (connection) before boarding. All passengers on that booking follow the same path.
TCP header is like a detailed shipping manifest (20+ fields for tracking), UDP is like a simple address label (only 4 fields).

analogies.analogy: Like sending individual letters through mail - each letter is independent, has full address, and may take different routes. No prior arrangement needed.
Like making a phone call - you dial (establish connection), talk (data transfer), then say goodbye (terminate). The path is established for the entire conversation.
UDP is like a announcement system - you speak and hope people hear. TCP is like registered mail with delivery confirmation.
Like a ongoing conversation where both parties remember what's been said (sequence numbers) and expect responses (acknowledgments).
